UserProfile.title: UserProfile
UserProfile.description: Represents a registered user of the Base64 Forge application, storing essential profile information.

id.description: Unique identifier for the UserProfile entity.

email.description: The user's email address, used for authentication and communication.

displayName.description: The public name chosen by the user or provided by a social login service.

profilePictureUrl.description: URL to the user's profile picture, if available.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the user profile was created.

ConversionSnippet.title: ConversionSnippet
ConversionSnippet.description: Represents a Base64 conversion result saved by a user, allowing them to revisit or reuse their generated code snippets.

id.description: Unique identifier for the ConversionSnippet entity.

userId.description: Reference to the UserProfile who saved this snippet. (Relationship: UserProfile 1:N ConversionSnippet)

fileName.description: The original file name of the image that was converted.

base64String.description: The generated Base64 encoded string, potentially truncated for storage efficiency.

mimeType.description: The MIME type of the original image (e.g., 'image/png'), useful for reconstructing data URIs.

outputFormat.description: The specific output format the user chose to save (e.g., 'raw', 'html', 'css', 'data-uri').

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when this snippet was saved.

definition.description: Stores individual user profile information, primarily for authentication context and display. Access is restricted to the authenticated user whose `uid` matches `{userId}`.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique ID of the user, matching `request.auth.uid`.

definition.description: Stores Base64 conversion snippets saved by a specific user. Each snippet document includes the `userId` field, which is denormalized from the parent path for authorization independence. Only the owning user can access their snippets.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique ID of the user who owns this snippet, matching `request.auth.uid` and `resource.data.userId`.

params.name: snippetId
params.description: The unique ID of the saved Base64 conversion snippet.

firestore.reasoning:
The Firestore data structure is designed with a clear focus on user-centric private data, adhering strictly to the core design principles of Authorization Independence, Structural Segregation, and Access Modeling. We will utilize two primary collections: 'users' for user profiles and a subcollection 'conversionSnippets' nested under each user for their saved Base64 content.

**Authorization Independence (Denormalization Strategy):**
Each `ConversionSnippet` document will be stored within a user-specific subcollection: `/users/{userId}/conversionSnippets/{snippetId}`. Crucially, every `ConversionSnippet` document will explicitly contain a `userId` field, matching both the parent `UserProfile`'s ID and the `{userId}` wildcard in its own path. This denormalization is vital: when a security rule evaluates access to a `ConversionSnippet` document, it can check `request.auth.uid == resource.data.userId` (or `request.auth.uid == userId` from the path wildcard) directly on the document's data without needing to perform a `get()` operation on the parent `UserProfile` document. This eliminates hierarchical authorization dependencies, enabling atomic writes (e.g., creating a user and their first snippet in a single batch) and significantly simplifying security rules. This makes rules robust and easy to debug.

**Querying and Authorization Posture (QAPs):**
This structure directly supports secure `list` operations (Query-as-a-Privilege) without the need for client-side filtering or complex server-side logic. For example, a user requesting their conversion history would query `/users/{request.auth.uid}/conversionSnippets`. The Firestore security rules can then enforce that the `{userId}` wildcard in the path precisely matches `request.auth.uid`. This ensures that a user can only query their own `conversionSnippets` subcollection. Any attempt to list snippets belonging to another user, or to list snippets from an unauthenticated context, will be blocked by the rules *before* any data is returned, thus upholding the principle that 'rules are not filters'. This structural approach ensures that only authorized `list` queries are ever processed by the database, making the application inherently more secure and performant.
